label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so there's this problem about a teacher and four students. The teacher picks four consecutive two-digit integers, gives each student a different number from this set, and tells them that one number is divisible by 6 and another by 7. Then, when asked if they can figure out the set, all four say no. But after hearing that, they all can figure it out. We need to find the sum of all possible greatest elements in such sets S. First, let me parse the problem again to make sure I understand. The set S has four consecutive two-digit numbers. So numbers like 10-13, 11-14, ..., up to maybe 97-99 (but since they are consecutive, the highest possible set would be something like 97,98,99,100 but 100 isn't two-digit. So the max would be 97,98,99, but that's only three numbers. Wait, four consecutive two-digit numbers. So the maximum set would be 97,98,99,100? But 100 is not two-digit. Hmm, so actually the four consecutive numbers must all be two-digit, so the maximum starting number would be 97, since 97,98,99,100—but 100 is excluded. Therefore, the four consecutive numbers must be between 10-99, so the starting number can be from 10 up to 96, because 96,97,98,99 are all two-digit numbers. So S is {n, n+1, n+2, n+3} for some n where 10 ≤ n ≤ 96. Also, in S, one number is divisible by 6, and another (different) number is divisible by 7. So exactly one multiple of 6 and exactly one multiple of 7? Or at least? The problem says "some number in S was divisible by 6, and a different number in S was divisible by 7." So "some" and "a different", so at least one multiple of 6 and at least one multiple of 7, but they could be more? Wait, but since the numbers are consecutive, there can't be more than one multiple of 6 or 7 in four consecutive numbers? Let me check. For multiples of 6: The gap between multiples of 6 is 6, so in four consecutive numbers, there can be at most one multiple of 6. Similarly for multiples of 7, since the gap is 7, in four consecutive numbers, there can be at most one multiple of 7. Therefore, the teacher's statement actually means that there is exactly one multiple of 6 and exactly one multiple of 7 in S. So the set S must contain exactly one multiple of 6 and exactly one multiple of 7. Therefore, our first job is to find
